artifacts.description: Run log documenting dataset screening and the reason no empirical estimate was produced.
Table summarizing suitability of each provided dataset hint for the user's research question.

limitations: No provided dataset hint could be verified in this run as containing both e-cigarette-use and epilepsy variables for women under age 50 in a population sample.
The BRFSS 2015 GitHub mirror available through the hint appears to be a diabetes-focused derived dataset, not the full BRFSS instrument needed for this question.

The other supplied datasets were outside scope for the user's epidemiologic question: MAST is astronomy, DANDI is neuroscience archive metadata rather than population tobacco surveillance, and NCI GDC is oncology-focused.
The Python execution environment in this run could not directly download internet-hosted files, so I could not materialize and inspect candidate remote CSV contents locally after discovery.
Because meaningful original estimation was not possible with the available materials, returning numeric prevalence or association results would have been misleading.
methods: I converted the user's idea into a testable cross-sectional epidemiology question, then evaluated the supplied dataset hints for fitness to answer it. I searched the web for the BRFSS 2015 GitHub mirror named in the hints and inspected accessible repository metadata and dataset descriptions to determine whether the mirrored file exposed variables for e-cigarette use and epilepsy. I also assessed the other provided data hints (MAST, DANDI, NCI GDC) for topical relevance to a population-level question about e-cigarette prevalence and epilepsy in women under age 50. I created and saved a run log and a source suitability table documenting this screening process. I did not fabricate estimates or run unsupported models because no provided source in this run could be verified as containing both target variables in an analyzable form within the available environment.
research_question: Using the datasets provided in this run, can we estimate the prevalence of e-cigarette use among women younger than 50 years and test whether e-cigarette use is associated with epilepsy?

sources.label: GitHub repository page indicating the accessible BRFSS mirror is a diabetes-focused derived dataset rather than a full BRFSS file with e-cigarette and epilepsy variables
sources.notes: Repository README states the project uses a Kaggle Diabetes Health Indicators dataset derived from CDC BRFSS 2015 with diabetes-related predictors such as BMI, smoking, physical activity, blood pressure, and general health.

api_query.q: BRFSS 2015 Diabetes Health Indicators raw csv ecig epilepsy women under 50 github mirror raw.githubusercontent.com

sources.label: Web search results for BRFSS 2015 diabetes mirror and related descriptions
sources.notes: Search results consistently described the mirrored dataset as a diabetes health indicators dataset with a limited set of derived variables, not a full tobacco-and-neurology surveillance file.

title: Feasibility assessment of estimating e-cigarette prevalence in women under 50 and its association with epilepsy using the provided data hints
